1: “Some time later Joseph was told, ‘Your father is ill.’” The vague “some time later” indicates narrative compression; the seventeen Egypt years pass quickly in the text (Genesis 47:28). Joseph takes both sons to Jacob’s bedside—Manasseh and Ephraim will be the objects of the blessing scene. Bringing them specifically signals that Joseph has a purpose for this visit beyond filial duty: he intends for his father to bless his Egyptian-born sons and thereby incorporate them into the covenant family.
2: Jacob “rallied his strength and sat up on the bed” at news of Joseph’s arrival. The act of sitting up from a deathbed is an expression of honor toward the visitor — in ANE and later Jewish etiquette, rising or sitting up for an honored person was a physical demonstration of respect even when physically difficult. The text notes Jacob’s physical effort (wayyitḥazzēq yiśrāʾēl, “Israel strengthened himself”) to signal that what follows is not casual conversation but a formal, deliberate act requiring full cognitive and spiritual presence.
3: Jacob invokes his most recent theophany: “God Almighty appeared to me at Luz in the land of Canaan.” Luz = Bethel (Genesis 28:19; Genesis 35:6). The invocation of ʾĒl Šaddāy (God Almighty, the patriarchal name for God before the Mosaic revelation of YHWH, Exodus 6:3) connects this blessing to the covenant framework established at Bethel. Jacob is not acting from personal sentiment but from divine mandate: the blessing he is about to give Ephraim and Manasseh is grounded in the Bethel covenant and is therefore valid regardless of Jacob’s personal preferences or family politics.
4: Jacob recites the Bethel blessing: “I will make you fruitful and increase your numbers. I will make you a community of peoples, and I will give this land as an everlasting possession to your descendants after you.” This is the Abrahamic covenant formula (Genesis 12:2; Genesis 17:6–8) now active in Jacob’s deathbed blessing. The covenant promises that Jacob received at Bethel are the authorization for what he is about to do: adopting Joseph’s sons extends the covenant line, creates two additional tribes in the land promise, and anchors everything in divine initiative rather than human decision.
5: The adoption formula: “Your two sons born to you in Egypt before I came to you here will be reckoned as mine; Ephraim and Manasseh will be mine, just as Reuben and Simeon are mine.” In ANE adoption law (attested in Nuzi tablets, Old Babylonian texts, and Hammurabi’s code), an adopting father’s declaration “you are my son” or “he is as my son” was the operative legal formula that transferred inheritance rights. Jacob’s declaration is a formal adoption that makes Manasseh and Ephraim full tribal patriarchs equivalent to Jacob’s biological sons. Joseph receives a double portion (two tribes instead of one) — the legal right of the firstborn (Deuteronomy 21:17), given to Joseph as the firstborn of Rachel, Jacob’s beloved wife.
6: “Any children born to you after them will be yours; in the territory they inherit they will be reckoned under the names of their brothers.” The legal precision is significant: Joseph could have additional sons (he is approximately 56 at this point), and their status is clearly defined. They will be subordinate to the two adopted sons and counted within Ephraim or Manasseh’s tribal territory rather than receiving independent tribal allotments. The adoption of only Manasseh and Ephraim is a deliberate limitation that creates exactly the tribal structure Israel will have: twelve tribes with Joseph replaced by Ephraim + Manasseh, and Levi receiving no territorial allotment (as the priestly tribe).
7: Jacob’s explanation for the special treatment of Ephraim and Manasseh is unexpected: he invokes Rachel’s death. “As I was returning from Paddan, to my sorrow Rachel died…” By giving Joseph a double portion (two tribes), Jacob is compensating posthumously for Rachel — the wife he loved most, who died before inheriting with Jacob in Canaan, whose sons were taken from him. The connection to Rachel’s burial place near Bethlehem is a geographical marker that links this deathbed scene to the covenant landscape of Canaan. Bethlehem will later be the city of David and the birthplace of Jesus.
8: “When Israel saw the sons of Joseph, he asked, ‘Who are these?’” Jacob’s question is surprising given that Manasseh and Ephraim have lived in Egypt for years and Jacob has been in Egypt seventeen years. The question is either a test of the formal adoption ritual (requiring Joseph to formally present his sons as petitioning for blessing), or it reflects Jacob’s failing eyesight (noted in v10). In ANE adoption ceremonies, formal presentation of the adoptee to the adopting father was part of the legal procedure; Jacob’s question may initiate the formal presentation sequence.
9: Joseph’s answer — “They are the sons God has given me here” — acknowledges both the divine gift and the Egyptian location (“here”). Jacob’s response — “Bring them to me so I may bless them” — is the patriarch’s formal acceptance of the presentation. The blessing by the patriarch was a legal act in ANE kinship law: the father’s blessing conferred status, inheritance rights, and covenant standing. Isaac’s blessing of Jacob and Esau (Genesis 27) shows that the patriarchal blessing was legally binding and irrevocable once given. Jacob is about to perform the same irrevocable legal act for Joseph’s sons.
10: “Israel’s eyes were failing because of old age, and he could hardly see.” This detail is not merely biographical; it echoes the narrative of Isaac’s blind eyes in Genesis 27, the deception in which Jacob received the firstborn blessing through a reversal of birth-order. Now Jacob himself has failing eyes — but unlike Isaac, who was deceived into giving the wrong blessing, Jacob will deliberately and knowingly cross his hands to give the younger son the greater blessing. The irony is precise: the son who received blessing through deception of a blind father will give blessing through deliberate action despite his own failing sight.
11: “I never expected to see your face again, and now God has allowed me to see your children too.” Jacob’s gratitude is framed entirely in terms of divine gift: the reunion with Joseph, the sight of his grandsons, are more than he deserved or expected. The phrase “God has allowed me to see” (hirʾāh ʾōtî ʾĕlōhîm) — literally “God has shown me” — attributes his survival and the reunion entirely to divine provision. The statement also acknowledges that Jacob is near death: seeing the grandsons is a final gift before closing his eyes.
12: Joseph’s prostration “with his face to the ground” before Jacob is a formal gesture of filial reverence appropriate to the gravity of the blessing ceremony. “He removed them from Israel’s knees” — the grandchildren had been sitting on Jacob’s knees, the posture of adoption in ANE custom (cf. Genesis 30:3 where Bilhah’s children are “born on Rachel’s knees” = adopted by Rachel). Joseph removes them from the adoption-posture and positions them for the blessing, with Manasseh (firstborn) placed to receive Jacob’s right hand and Ephraim (younger) placed to receive Jacob’s left hand.
13: Joseph’s careful positioning — Ephraim on his right toward Israel’s left, Manasseh on his left toward Israel’s right — follows the expected protocol: the firstborn Manasseh should receive the right hand (the stronger blessing hand). In ANE symbolic gesture, the right hand conveyed greater blessing, authority, and status; the left hand was the lesser (cf. Matthew 25:33 right/left distinction). Joseph has done everything correctly according to birth-order protocol. Jacob is about to deliberately reverse it.
14: “Israel reached out his right hand and put it on Ephraim’s head, though he was the younger, and crossing his arms—” The Hebrew verb for “crossing” (śikkēl, Piel of śākal) means “to act wisely/prudently” or “to cross deliberately.” The Piel form is intensive: Jacob crossed his hands with full intentional wisdom, not by accident or confusion (his eyes were poor but his mind was clear). The same root will appear in v19 when he insists he knows what he is doing (“I know, my son, I know”). This is the reversal of firstborn priority — the same pattern that runs through Genesis: the younger chosen over the older (Abel/Cain in God’s regard, Isaac/Ishmael, Jacob/Esau, Joseph/Reuben).
15: The blessing invokes God under three complementary descriptions: “the God before whom my fathers Abraham and Isaac walked” — the covenantal God of the patriarchal promise-history; “the God who has been my shepherd all my life to this day” — the personal providential God who has guided Jacob; and (v16) “the Angel who has delivered me from all harm” — the mediating divine figure who intervened personally (cf. the wrestling angel of Genesis 32:24–30). The three-part divine description is not a trinitarian formula but a comprehensive invocation of God as covenant-keeper, shepherd, and personal redeemer — all three aspects participating in the blessing.
16: “The Angel who has delivered me from all harm” (hammalʾāk haggōʾēl ʾōtî mikkol-rāʿ) — the term gōʾēl is the kinsman-redeemer, the family member obligated to rescue a relative from slavery, debt, or danger (Leviticus 25:25; Ruth 2:20; Genesis 3:12). Applying this term to a divine “angel” who redeems Jacob is theologically significant: God functions as Jacob’s kinsman, taking on the role of family-redeemer on Jacob’s behalf. “May they be called by my name and the names of my fathers Abraham and Isaac” — the naming formula signals full legal adoption into the covenant line; the boys’ Egyptian birth does not prevent them from bearing the patriarchs’ covenantal identity.
17: Joseph’s displeasure and his attempt to correct his father’s hands is the narrative’s moment of dramatic tension. Joseph has managed his family’s affairs in Egypt with political precision; here he tries to “manage” his father’s blessing according to proper protocol. His hand on Jacob’s hand is a filial intervention, physically trying to redirect the blessing. The scene parallels Isaac’s discovery that he had blessed the wrong son — but here, the patriarch acts knowingly and the son’s intervention fails. Joseph cannot override his father’s deliberate prophetic act.
18: “No, my father, this one is the firstborn; put your right hand on his head.” Joseph’s protest is legalistic — he invokes the firstborn-priority rule as the grounds for correction. His understanding of how blessing should work is shaped by inheritance law and social convention. Jacob’s response transcends those conventions: the covenant has always moved through chosen rather than natural succession. Joseph knows the history but apparently believes his sons will be an exception to the pattern.
19: “I know, my son, I know. He too will become a people, and he too will become great. Nevertheless, his younger brother will be greater than he, and his descendants will become a group of nations.” The repeated “I know” (yāḏaʿtî bĕnî yāḏaʿtî) is emphatic reassurance and mild rebuke simultaneously: I am not mistaken, I am acting in full awareness of what I am doing. “A group of nations” (mĕlōʾ haggôyim, “fullness of nations”) — the phrase anticipates Ephraim’s descendants becoming the dominant force in the northern ten tribes and eventually the largest single tribal territory. Ephraim became so prominent that the northern kingdom was called “Ephraim” in prophetic literature (Isaiah 7:2; Hosea 5:3).
20: “In your name will Israel pronounce this blessing: ‘May God make you like Ephraim and Manasseh.’” The blessing becomes proverbial: future Israelite blessing-formulas would invoke Ephraim before Manasseh as the standard of divine favor. The reversal of birth-order is institutionalized in the liturgical formula itself — every Israelite father who uses this blessing acknowledges that God’s election overrides human convention. “So he put Ephraim ahead of Manasseh” — the narrator’s closing note confirms the order is permanent, not provisional.
21: “I am about to die, but God will be with you and take you back to the land of your fathers.” Jacob’s dying word to Joseph is a covenant promise rather than personal advice: the God who went down with Jacob to Egypt will bring Joseph and his descendants back to Canaan. The promise anticipates the Exodus without naming it. Joseph will take this prophecy seriously enough to make the Israelites swear to carry his bones back to Canaan (Genesis 50:24–25), an oath fulfilled in Joshua 24:32. The “God will be with you” assurance echoes the theophanic assurance of 46:4 and the entire pattern of divine accompaniment in Genesis.
22: “To you I give one more ridge of land than to your brothers, the ridge I took from the Amorites with my sword and my bow.” The Hebrew šĕkem ʾaḥaḏ is literally “one shoulder/ridge/portion.” Šĕkem is also the name of the city Shechem — a deliberate wordplay. Jacob may be referring to the land purchased at Shechem (Genesis 33:19) or to a military action not otherwise recorded. John 4:5 locates the “plot of ground Jacob had given to his son Joseph” near Sychar (a variant of Shechem), connecting this verse to the geography of Jesus’ encounter with the Samaritan woman. The double meaning — literal land-grant plus Shechem-wordplay — gives the verse both concrete legal and symbolic resonance.
